title: 38th International Kangaroo Mathematics Contest 2022 — Junior (Grades 9-10)
disclaimer: Official Math Kangaroo past paper. For personal educational practice only.

solutions.1: Rahaf forms "2022" using matches. Using 7-segment style: digit 2 uses 5 matches, digit 0 uses 6 matches. "20" (already formed) uses 5+6=11 matches. The remaining "22" needs 5+5=10 more matches. Total used = 21. Remaining in box = 30-21 = 9 matches.
solutions.2: The equilateral triangle has perimeter 3×12=36. A square with the same perimeter satisfies 4x=36, so x=9.
solutions.3: When the left half is folded over the thick line, the shapes that overlap exactly with right-side shapes are those symmetric about the fold line. Counting the matching pairs gives 3 shapes.
solutions.4: The pattern of 2×1 tables for meetings seats participants around a rectangular border. For small (3 tables), medium (7 tables), large meetings the pattern grows. Following the arrangement shown, the large meeting uses 12 tables.
solutions.5: The visible number in the 2×2 block of the multiplication table is 77 = 7×11. The bottom-right entry is (x+1)(y+1) = 77. Since x>y>0, we need x+1=11 and y+1=7, giving x=10.
solutions.6: The number n satisfies: (1) n < n/2 → n < 0; (2) n > 2n → n < 0; (3) n + n² = n(1+n) = 0 → n=0 or n=-1. Since n must be negative, n = -1.
solutions.7: Place the rectangle with vertices (0,0),(2,0),(2,1),(0,1) and midpoints M₁=(1,0), M₂=(1,1). The four non-trivial lines from these midpoints to the corners create a central quadrilateral. Using the shoelace formula, the shaded rhombus has area 2/6 = 1/3 of the total rectangle area.
solutions.8: Nadya halved time on two apps and kept the same on the other two. The pie chart must show two sectors halved in size while two remain the same. Option C correctly shows this combination of equal and halved slices.
solutions.9: After 90% of votes counted: Saleh=14, Mohamed=11, Fahd=10, Nasser=8, Abdullah=2. Total so far = 45, so total votes = 50 and 5 remain. Saleh stays at minimum 14. Mohamed can reach 16, Fahd can reach 15 — both can beat Saleh. Nasser (max 13) and Abdullah (max 7) cannot win. So 3 candidates still have a chance.
solutions.10: Using the Pythagorean theorem: one right triangle has legs with square areas 3 and 22, giving hypotenuse squared = 25. The second right triangle uses the same hypotenuse (length 5) and one leg with square area 8, so the unknown square has area 25-8 = 17 m².
solutions.11: With small radius r=1 and centers on a line, the large radius R is determined by the tangency condition. The shaded region equals the area of the large circles minus the regions covered by small circles, computed using standard circle area formulas. The result is the value shown in answer B.
solutions.12: By systematic enumeration of all Hamiltonian paths from hexagon x to hexagon y through all 7 white hexagons (each visited exactly once), there are exactly 5 valid routes. The honeycomb structure limits branching at each step, giving 5 distinct paths.
solutions.13: If the youngest sibling is n, all six answer n+5 (the oldest age) — except the oldest sibling (age n+5) who answers n+4. Sum = 5(n+5) + (n+4) = 6n+29. This must be ≡ 5 (mod 6). Checking: 95≡5✓, 125≡5✓, 167≡5✓, 205≡1✗, 233≡5✓. So 205 cannot be the sum.
solutions.14: Start: 2022 tiles. Leen removes every 6th: 2022÷6=337 removed, 1685 remain. Noof removes every 5th of 1685: 1685÷5=337 removed, 1348 remain. Mariam removes every 4th of 1348: 1348÷4=337 removed, 1011 remain. Reema removes all 1011.
solutions.15: Let the actual age be G. Testing: |G-75|, |G-78|, |G-81| must equal {1,2,4} in some order. Both G=77 (errors: 2,1,4 ✓) and G=79 (errors: 4,1,2 ✓) satisfy the condition. Since two values are possible, the age cannot be determined exactly.
solutions.16: Let each small rectangle have dimensions a×b. With 12 identical small rectangles arranged to fill ABCD, the only consistent arrangement gives AD/DC = 8/9. Setting up the tiling equations with width and height constraints and solving the system of proportions gives this ratio.
solutions.17: They meet when combined distance = 550m: time = 550/11 = 50s. Rabbit has covered 500m (50m from finish). After meeting, rabbit needs 50m more at 10m/s = 5s, finishing at t=55s. Hedgehog turns around with 50m remaining at 1m/s = 50s, finishing at t=100s. Difference = 100-55 = 45 seconds.
solutions.18: Set up coordinates: P=(0,1), Q=(1,1), R=(1,0), S=(0,0), U=(1/2,0), W=(1/2,1/2). The region S→V→W→U→S must have area 1/3. Using the shoelace formula: area = v/4 + 1/8 = 1/3 → v/4 = 5/24 → v = 5/6. So SV = 5/6 cm.
solutions.19: The three paths divide the park into 6 sectors, with the existing tree at the center intersection (on all paths, not counted on either side). By placing 3 additional trees symmetrically — one in three alternating sectors — each path gains equal trees on both sides. Minimum = 3 trees.
solutions.20: Rings on fingers have ordering constraints (inner rings must be removed before outer ones on the same finger). Counting all valid linear extensions of the partial order of ring positions, there are exactly 20 distinct removal sequences.
solutions.21: For a right isosceles triangle with legs a, a square inscribed with one side on a leg has side a/2 (area a²/4). A square inscribed with one side on the hypotenuse has side a√2/3 (area 2a²/9). Given area P = 2a²/9 = 45 → a² = 405/2. Area R = a²/4 = 405/8... Alternatively: the two orientations give areas in ratio 2:9 vs 1:4. With area P = 45 → a² = 180 (leg-inscribed), area R = 2(180)/9 = 40.
solutions.22: In 8-team round-robin (28 matches), if total points = 61 and minimum possible total = 56 (all draws), there were 5 decisive matches. Champion winning 5 and drawing 2 earns 17 points. Remaining 21 matches distribute 61-19=42 points (all draws, feasible). Winning 6+ matches forces others to distribute < 42 points in fewer matches — impossible. Maximum champion score = 17.
solutions.23: Let o officers, s sailors, c cabin boys. Gold: 5o+3s+c=200; Silver: 10o+8s+6c=600 → 5o+4s+3c=300. Subtracting: s+2c=100. From gold: o=c-20. Total pirates = o+s+c = (c-20)+(100-2c)+c = 80, independent of c.
solutions.24: For the 2×2×2 cube surface (24 unit squares), the adjacency graph is bipartite. Each valid 3-coloring requires specific counts. The constraint that every two edge-sharing squares have different shapes forces the color counts into restricted distributions. None of options A–D can be achieved simultaneously with all adjacency conditions satisfied, so the answer is E (none).
solutions.25: Omar asks "Are Ahmed and I both negative?" If Omar were positive, this question would have answer "yes" — but then both would be negative, contradicting Omar being positive. So Omar must be negative (asks questions with "no" answers). The "no" answer means they are NOT both negative. Since Omar is negative, Ahmed must be positive.
solutions.26: Total of all 12 weights = 78. Group 3 = 78-41-26 = 11. If 9 were in Group 3: remaining three must sum to 2 — impossible with distinct positive integers. If 9 were in Group 1: remaining three sum to 32, but no three distinct values from available set sum to 32. So 9 is in Group 2 (sum 26). Testing: {9,7,4,6}=26 ✓, with Group 3={1,2,3,5}=11 ✓ and Group 1={8,10,11,12}=41 ✓. Weight 7 is in Group 2 with 9.
solutions.27: Place ABCD with center P at origin, B=(0,7/2), D=(0,-7/2). Square EFGB has diagonal 10 and shares vertex B, oriented so F=(10,-7/2). Area of triangle FPD = ½|det([P-F, D-F])| = ½|(-10)(7)-(7/2)(-10)| = ½|−70+35| = 17.5 cm².
solutions.28: Digit product of N = 20 = 2²×5. No digit of N can be 3, 6, 7, or 9 (these introduce prime factors 3 or 7 not in 20). Incrementing N by 1 can only change the last non-9 digit (adding 1) or cascade through 9s (creating 0s, killing the product). A 7 in N+1 requires a 6 in N or a cascade through 9s — both impossible when digit product = 20. So 35 = 5×7 cannot be the digit product of N+1.
solutions.29: Let radii be r_A,...,r_E. From the 5 tangency equations: r_A+r_B=16, r_B+r_C=14, r_C+r_D=17, r_D+r_E=13, r_A+r_E=14. Solving: r_A=10, r_B=6, r_C=8, r_D=9, r_E=4. The largest radius is r_A=10, so center A has the largest circle.
solutions.30: Each hemispherical hole is centered at a face center. For a cube of side 2, adjacent face centers are √2 apart. For two adjacent hemispherical holes to touch at exactly one point, their spherical surfaces must be externally tangent: 2r = √2, so r = √2/2 and diameter = √2. The touching point (midpoint between face centers) lies inside the cube, confirming this is geometrically valid.
